rationale_for_baseline_tier: A gaping ~4 cm dermis-deep pretibial laceration with active but venous, compressible bleeding needs urgent SAME-DAY in-person professional cleaning and primary closure to limit infection and achieve good healing — something telehealth cannot provide. The bleeding is controllable with direct pressure, distal neurovascular function is intact, brief weight-bearing is possible, there is no chest pain, no prodrome/LOC and no head strike, and the tachycardia/hypertension are proportional to acute pain and panic. There is therefore no red flag mandating an ambulance, but the wound cannot safely wait for a routine appointment — hence T3 (urgent same-day / urgent care), not T4/T5 and not routine GP. The panic is situational and settles with reassurance.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Bleeding that soaks through the dressing, will not stop after ~10–15 minutes of firm continuous pressure, or is spurting
escalation_edges.rationale: Uncontrolled or arterial haemorrhage is an immediate threat to life and requires an emergency ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the bleeding soaks through, won't stop with firm pressure, or starts spurting, keep pressing hard and call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Feeling faint, dizzy, very pale or clammy beyond the initial fright, or worsening weakness (possible significant blood loss)
escalation_edges.rationale: Signs of haemodynamic compromise from blood loss require emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she starts feeling faint, goes very pale or clammy, or feels weak and unwell, lay her down, keep pressure on the wound and call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Inability to move or bear any weight, numbness or a cold/pale foot, or an obvious deformity of the leg
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests fracture or neurovascular/tendon injury needing emergency department assessment and imaging.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she can't move the leg or foot at all, the foot goes numb or cold, or the leg looks deformed, go to the emergency department urgently.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New light-headedness, palpitations, or a further collapse suggesting a non-mechanical cause
escalation_edges.rationale: Would reopen concern for a syncopal/cardiac cause of falls needing urgent assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she feels faint or her heart races out of the blue, or she collapses again, get urgent medical assessment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any new headache, vomiting, drowsiness or confusion (delayed head-injury signs)
escalation_edges.rationale: Even though no head strike is reported, new neurological symptoms after a fall warrant urgent assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she develops a bad headache, vomiting, unusual drowsiness or confusion, treat it as urgent and go to the emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Chest pain or tightness accompanying the racing heart
escalation_edges.rationale: Chest pain would change the picture from panic toward a possible cardiac cause requiring urgent assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she gets any chest pain or tightness with the racing heart, don't put it down to panic — seek urgent care.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Signs of wound infection over the following days — spreading redness or warmth, swelling, pus, increasing pain, or fever
escalation_edges.rationale: Wound infection needs prompt same-day medical review; systemic features (fever, feeling very unwell) warrant escalation to ED.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Over the next few days, if the wound gets more red, hot, swollen or painful, leaks pus, or you get a fever, see a doctor the same day — and go to the emergency department if you feel very unwell.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Congenital blindness — cannot visually self-inspect the wound
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: All wound-monitoring and infection safety-netting must be routed through the sighted spouse/carer, with explicit tactile and descriptive cues rather than 'watch for redness'.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Relies on the spouse and normally a guide dog (currently shut away); acute disorientation from the changed layout
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Ensure the spouse can arrange and accompany supported transport for in-person closure, and that the environment is restored and the guide dog reunited before independent mobility resumes.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Profound spatial disorientation makes independent movement unsafe right now
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Advise the patient to stay put with the spouse's help rather than attempting to move to the phone/door unaided, reducing further fall risk while awaiting care.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Keep firm, continuous pressure on the cut with a clean cloth and raise the leg to slow the bleeding. Arrange to be seen in person today for the wound to be cleaned and closed — a same-day GP, urgent care clinic or nurse-led wound service. Stay sitting or lying with your spouse's help and do the slow-breathing routine to settle the panic. If the bleeding won't stop with pressure, soaks through, or you feel faint, call 000.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Same-day in-person GP / urgent care clinic for wound closure; call 000 for uncontrolled bleeding or faintness
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, use an after-hours GP or urgent care service for the closure, or the emergency department if bleeding cannot be controlled.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Mechanical trip over relocated furniture in a congenitally blind patient; ~4 cm gaping pretibial laceration with controllable venous bleeding; intact distal neurovascular function, brief weight-bearing possible; no chest pain, no prodrome/LOC, no head strike; tachycardia/hypertension proportional to pain and situational panic; tetanus up to date; urgent home-safety/orientation assessment arranged.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating a gaping, actively bleeding dermis-deep laceration as self-care or a routine appointment, leaving it unclosed.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Delayed closure leads to wound infection, poor healing and scarring, and an occult fracture/head injury or ongoing blood loss could be missed.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Booking a routine 24–48 hour GP appointment rather than same-day closure delays the wound beyond the optimal primary-closure window.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Dispatching an ambulance or directing to ED for a controllable venous ooze in a stable patient — resource-wasteful and a distressing, disorienting transport for a blind patient in panic.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot examine the wound depth or fully exclude an occult fracture or deep-structure injury.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Default to urgent same-day in-person assessment where the wound can be explored and closed, and safety-net explicitly for fracture/neurovascular and bleeding red flags.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either reassuring the patient to manage entirely at home, or over-reacting to the uncertainty with an emergency ambulance for a stable, controllable injury.
